prompt:
Training appears to make no progress: loss stays around the entropy-baseline for the vocabulary and tokens-per-second is suspiciously high. Script:
```python
from trl import SFTConfig, SFTTrainer
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
from datasets import Dataset

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained('TinyLlama/TinyLlama-1.1B-Chat-v1.0')
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained('TinyLlama/TinyLlama-1.1B-Chat-v1.0')
tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token
ds = Dataset.from_list([{'text': 'yes'}, {'text': 'no'}, {'text': 'maybe'}] * 1000)
args = SFTConfig(output_dir='./out', per_device_train_batch_size=4,
                 num_train_epochs=3, learning_rate=2e-4,
                 max_seq_length=4096, packing=True)
trainer = SFTTrainer(model=model, args=args, train_dataset=ds, tokenizer=tokenizer)
trainer.train()
```

criteria.description: Identifies the actual root cause, not a downstream symptom
Suggests a fix that would actually resolve it
Does not introduce a new bug in the fix
Explains why the bug happened (mechanistic understanding)

reference_solution:
Root cause: with `packing=True` and `max_seq_length=4096` on a dataset of 1-3-token samples, SFTTrainer concatenates many short examples into 4096-token sequences separated by EOS tokens. That's fine in principle - but the dataset has only 3 unique samples ('yes', 'no', 'maybe') repeated 1000 times each, which packing concatenates into a single near-degenerate token sequence (eos-yes-eos-no-eos-maybe-eos-yes-...). The model trains on predicting these tokens conditional on essentially-no-context, and the loss converges to log(p(token)) for the empirical token distribution - which IS the entropy baseline.

Fix: the script-level fix is dataset diversity, but assuming the user has a real corpus and the 3 samples are just illustrative, the configuration fix is to NOT pack tiny samples:
```python
args = SFTConfig(output_dir='./out', per_device_train_batch_size=4,
                 num_train_epochs=3, learning_rate=2e-4,
                 max_seq_length=512,  # don't pad/truncate to 4096 for ~3-token samples
                 packing=False)        # one sample per sequence
```
If packing is desired for throughput on a real diverse corpus, keep it on but make sure the corpus has enough variety to fill 4096-token windows meaningfully.

Mechanism: SFTTrainer's packing=True uses a ConstantLengthDataset under the hood that concatenates examples with EOS separators and emits chunks of exactly max_seq_length. It's a real throughput win on long-tail datasets (avoids padding waste) but it changes the training distribution: every token is now conditioned on a mix of multi-document context. When the documents are uniform (yes/no/maybe), the model can only learn the marginal token distribution. The high tokens-per-second is a tell: it means the trainer is processing very long sequences efficiently, but those sequences contain no signal beyond unigram frequencies.
